skill.name: developing-genkit-go
skill.description: Develop AI-powered applications using Genkit in Go. Use when the user asks to build AI features, agents, flows, or tools in Go using Genkit, or when working with Genkit Go code involving generation, prompts, streaming, tool calling, or model providers.
skill.summary: Build AI-powered applications with Genkit in Go. Covers generation, prompts, flows, tools, and model providers.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: All 283 static findings are false positives. This is a legitimate Firebase community skill providing Genkit Go SDK documentation. The skill contains Go code examples with printf-style string formatting (e.g., 'Tell me a joke about %s'), bash CLI installation commands, and references to standard AI API environment variable names. No malicious code patterns exist.

medium_findings.title: Static Scanner Pattern False Positive - Printf Formatting
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 241 locations but these are all Go code examples using printf-style string formatting (fmt.Printf pattern: 'message about %s', topic). This is standard Go string formatting syntax, not command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Go's fmt.Printf-style formatting with %s is clearly documented Go syntax, not backtick execution. Code example: ai.WithPrompt('Tell me a joke about %s', topic)

medium_findings.title: Static Scanner Pattern False Positive - Bash Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'external_commands' for curl commands used to install the Genkit CLI. Commands like 'curl -sL cli.genkit.dev | bash' are standard CLI installation patterns used by many legitimate tools including Homebrew, nvm, and official SDKs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: curl piping to bash for CLI installation is a standard documented pattern for the Genkit SDK, similar to other widely-used developer tools.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Pattern False Positive - Environment Variable References
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'env_access' for references to standard API key environment variable names (GEMINI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY). These are documented configuration settings for authenticating with AI providers, not actual secret access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard documentation of environment variable configuration for AI provider authentication - standard SDK setup documentation pattern.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Pattern False Positive - Hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs for AI provider documentation links (ai.google.dev, docs.anthropic.com) and localhost development addresses. These are legitimate reference URLs in documentation context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documentation reference URLs and localhost addresses in development guides are expected and legitimate.

content.user_title: Build AI Apps with Genkit Go SDK
content.value_statement: Building AI applications in Go requires understanding Genkit SDK patterns for generation, prompts, flows, and model providers. This skill provides comprehensive reference documentation for creating AI-powered Go applications with structured output, streaming, and tool calling.
actual_capabilities: Generate text and structured data using AI models in Go
Define reusable prompts with templates and schemas
Create AI flows with tracing, observability, and HTTP serving
Implement tool calling for multi-step AI workflows
Configure multiple model providers (Google AI, Vertex AI, Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible, Ollama)
Stream AI responses for real-time output display
limitations: Model provider API keys must be obtained separately from each provider
Does not include built-in authentication or user management

use_cases.title: Build AI-powered APIs
use_cases.description: Create HTTP APIs that use AI models for content generation, classification, or data extraction with built-in tracing.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building AI-enhanced web services

use_cases.title: Implement multi-step AI workflows
use_cases.description: Build complex AI flows with tool calling, where the model can invoke custom Go functions to gather information or perform actions.

use_cases.title: Generate structured data with AI
use_cases.description: Use Genkit's schema support to generate typed Go structs directly from AI model outputs, eliminating manual JSON parsing.
use_cases.target_user: Go developers needing type-safe AI integration

prompt_templates.title: Basic text generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use genkit.GenerateText to generate a joke about a topic the user provides.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants simple text generation from an AI model

prompt_templates.title: Structured output generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a flow that uses genkit.GenerateData with a Go struct schema to return structured JSON data.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user needs typed AI output that maps directly to Go types

prompt_templates.title: Prompt with tool calling
prompt_templates.prompt: Define a Genkit tool that allows the AI to search a database, then show how to use it in a flow.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants AI to interact with external systems

prompt_templates.title: Multi-provider setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Genkit with both Google AI and Anthropic providers, showing how to switch between models.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user needs flexibility to use different AI providers

output_examples.input: Create a flow that generates a joke about the topic 'programming bugs'
output_examples.output: A working Go file with genkit.Init, a DefineFlow for joke generation, HTTP handler setup with genkit.Handler, and instructions to test it with genkit start.

output_examples.input: Show how to generate structured JSON with a Recipe type
output_examples.output: Go code defining Recipe and Ingredient structs with jsonschema tags, then genkit.GenerateData call returning a *Recipe with title, ingredients, and steps fields.

best_practices: Pass the *Genkit instance explicitly through your call chain instead of storing it as a global variable
Use jsonschema struct tags on output types so models understand what each field should contain
Write clear tool descriptions - the model decides which tools to call based on these descriptions
anti_patterns: Avoid hardcoding model names - check provider documentation for current model IDs as they change frequently
Do not skip flows for production code - flows provide tracing and observability that are essential for debugging
Avoid vague tool descriptions - they lead to missed or incorrect tool calls from the model

faq.question: What model providers does Genkit Go support?
faq.answer: Genkit Go supports Google AI (Gemini), Vertex AI, Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI-compatible APIs, and Ollama for local models.

faq.question: How do I get started with Genkit Go?
faq.answer: Initialize a Go module, install the Genkit SDK with go get, add a provider plugin, then use genkit.Init to start building flows and prompts.

faq.question: What is the difference between GenerateText and Generate?
faq.answer: GenerateText returns only a string. Generate returns the full ModelResponse with metadata, usage stats, and conversation history.

faq.question: How do I use Claude Code with Genkit Go?
faq.answer: Genkit Go is designed to help you build AI applications that Claude Code can use. Reference this skill when creating Go code for AI-powered features.

faq.question: Can I stream AI responses in real-time?
faq.answer: Yes, use GenerateStream for text streaming or GenerateDataStream for streaming structured objects as they are generated.

faq.question: How do I test my Genkit flows?
faq.answer: Use the Genkit CLI with 'genkit start' to launch the Developer UI where you can run flows interactively, view traces, and inspect prompt rendering.
